data.title: Arteris: Unlocking Long-Term Value In The AI Semiconductor Ecosystem
data.description:
Introduction: The Undervalued Architect of AI's Future

In the wake of the 'revolutionary' AI chipmakers that scoop headlines and dominate the market, the investment community frequently undervalues enabling technologies - the technology that makes the advanced semiconductor technologies a reality. One of these enabling technology companies is Arteris, a key but often ignored player in the AI revolution. Arteris designs and licenses the interconnect "fabric" IP that connects multiple components in a system-on-chip and enables complex communication between them, a critical component for every modern AI, automotive, or data center processor.

My primary investment thesis is that the market is massively undervaluing Arteris (NASDAQ:AIP).

Investors seem focused on its current lack of GAAP profitability, failing to price in two key factors: the immense long-term value of its compounding royalty revenue model and its recent, pivotal endorsement from AMD. The market neglect of Arteris has also created an extraordinary opportunity for long-term investors to establish long-term positions in Arteris today. After conducting a thorough analysis of Arteris' financial situation, identifying growth catalysts, and using a conservative peer-based valuation, I am rating Arteris as a Strong Buy with a price target of $23.00 per share.

Company Background

Picture a state-of-the-art, ultra-complicated microchip as a city. The city has various neighborhoods, a central processing unit [CPU] as city hall, a graphics processing unit [GPU] as the art district, artificial intelligence processors (NPUs) as the advanced thinking district, and memory banks as the library in our city. For this city to function, it must have a complicated highway system to travel billions of pieces of information instantly between neighborhoods without traffic. Arteris builds that digital highway system.

Arteris does not manufacture the chips. Rather, as described in its May 2025 Corporate Overview, Arteris designs and licenses all the essential plans (the Intellectual Property) to build an on-chip network, or Network-on-Chip (NoC). Now, as chips for AI and autonomous driving become exponentially more complex with hundreds of processing engines on a piece of silicon, a highly efficient and intelligent "traffic control" system is no longer a nice-to-have; it has become an absolute need. Arteris's technology helps get data to where it needs to go with minimal time and energy consumption, which is essential for the next generation of electronics to perform.

The business model is very powerful and scoped for long-term compounding growth. Arteris is a two-phase business.

License Fees: A customer, such as a major semiconductor company, pays Arteris a one-time upfront fee to get authorized to use Arteris's NoC design tools & plans. This initial payment generates the first revenue stream and covers the cost of development and professional services.

Royalties: This is the really valuable part of the business. When the customer integrates Arteris's IP into their final chip design and goes to mass production, the customer pays Arteris a small fee for every single unit they release into the stream of commerce. Whether a chip goes into a new car, data center server, or smartphone, Arteris gets their cut. This creates a long-tail, high-margin, recurring revenue stream that could last for many years, long after the initial design work is concluded.

Arteris is the dominant player in its space, especially in the demanding automotive and AI spaces. The company's technology is already included in billions of chips globally. Arteris is a foundational technology partner, often invisible, to the world's most prominent technology companies.

Financials

A fair review of Arteris's financial condition reflects a classic investment-for-growth story where Arteris has a strong balance sheet and attractive legal ownership business model but is producing large operating losses, amidst investments in expansion. The strength of this balance sheet is central to the investment thesis. It provides a financial cushion that allows Arteris to absorb near-term losses from its strategic investments.

Balance Sheet

Based on Arteris's Q1 2025 balance sheet, the company has substantial cash and equivalents on its balance sheet and near-zero long-term debt. This clean balance sheet provides the company substantial operating flexibility and protects it from any interest rate variability. Additionally, it provides a runway for the company to fund its operations and strategic investments without going to the capital markets to fund operations and further dilute shareholder value. The financial backdrop is a very effective de-risking component that allows the company to focus its efforts on tech development and seizing market share.

Income Statement

A look at the income statement tells two stories. The top-line story tells the story of the attractiveness of the IP licensing model. Qualitative advantages associated with legal ownership of an IP model have permitted the company to produce gross margins just above 90%, as disclosed in the notes to the Q2 Earnings Report. Revenues comprise up-front license fees from the license fees typically disclosed under revenue recognition, and ongoing royalties. Revenues have primarily been from license fees, and we are in the formal transition to the long-tail royalty business and continue to be in the early innings of the licensing game.

Above the gross profit line, all is clear sailing, but below the gross profit line tells a different story. The company has not historically been GAAP profitable due to high and deliberate operating costs (intentional, as explained in earlier notes). The largest form of expense is the R&D expense, which is of not inconsequential size relative to total revenues. This statement is not a reflection of inefficiency; instead, it is a statement of strategy. The company is more historically investing today to create the next-generation IP that is being called for deals such as AMD that was just announced, with design wins being the first seeds towards the royalty payments streaming.

Sales and marketing expenses are also large with the company simply trying to grow the number of customers. Netted out, we have a glaring GAAP net loss. This net GAAP loss is the biggest "bad" on the minds of investors, but it is important to note that the total GAAP loss has to be viewed in the context of the "good" it is driving, which is a favorable optimal market position for the company, and a new and potentially large upstream revenue stream that will be flowing from royalty/usage based revenue streams.

Cash Flow

The cash flow statement is the most straightforward picture of the financial position, and it makes it very clear this company is burning cash. The cash burn from operations is the incremental cash cost of the growth strategy presented in the income statement. While the cash burn is a risk, it appears manageable when compared to the cash on the statement of financial position. The main takeaway is to have investors benchmark burn broadly to forward-looking relevant benchmarks such as the company's Annual Recurring Revenue [ARR] and Remaining Performance Obligation [RPO] which were indicated to be growing in the earnings call. Provided the investment (cash burn) can ultimately build a larger future revenue RPO backlog, then the plan is working. The risk is that cash burn continues until some future date, growth picks up, and cash flow positivity results for the company.

In summary, Arteris is presenting finances as a growth company and not as profit generating mature company. The company is attempting, as much as it can, to utilize a strong balance sheet and absorb short-term losses today to invest across the organization in R&D, build, and build the foundations in the AI semiconductor market.

Once accomplished, if all goes to plan, AIP can create extremely profitable and predictable royalty revenue going forward.

5 August 2025 Q2 Earnings Update

Follow up to our deep dive into Arteris, the recently released Q2 2025 earnings results from the company, and their recent investor presentation provide some important new color and even affirm the long-term thesis we spun out.

Arteris reported quarterly revenue of $16.5 million, up 13% year-over-year, to go along with notable new high-water marks for both annual contract value + royalties ($69.1 million) and remaining performance obligations ($99.3 million). These metrics clearly demonstrate the Arteris pipeline is on the ascent, especially in the A.I. and automotive verticals which we pointed out as the key catalytic growth drivers of the Arteris business model. While Arteris still loses money on a GAAP basis reporting a net loss of $7.8 million in the quarter, the healthy above average gross margins and mildly declining non-GAAP operating loss figures only support the viability of the high value low-cost IP licensing model we pitched. Financially the cash balances are solid, there is no material debt, and the company is well positioned to continue growth without meaningful dilution to shareholders.

The most recent investor presentation also pointed to several new developments which further strengthen the case for Arteris as a foundational company in the semiconductor ecosystem.

FlexGen Smart NoC IP roll out should provide meaningful contributions on the improved engineering productivity levels but also improved power consumption levels, while also providing Arteris some credibility as chip complexity accelerates. As we have seen with the new design wins with Infineon, GigaDevice, and Menta while continuing to deepen relationships with existing top-tier customers, Arteris is not just continuing to make inroads into the semiconductor ecosystem, but instead is becoming entrenched with the hottest next-generation chip designs. Momentum in the A.I. and automotive domains, aided by the recent AMD partnership, is indeed resulting in actual traction in the business.

That being said, Arteris stock was down more than 10% based on after-hours trading compared to where it was priced before the results were released. It appears that the stock move was a combination of disappointing full-year guidance, what will clearly be ongoing cash burn, and a regular 'sell the news' event after the recent rise in share price. Some investors have become impatient with the timing of profitability, despite the fundamentals being intact. The company continues to execute on the plans shared, the backlog is increasing in value, and the company is ramped up to execute on the long-term royalty compounding story we shared. Moreover, I believe the most recent results and business developments only better back up the view that Arteris is building a premium, high margin royalty-driven business at the heart of the AI and automotive chip revolution. For long-term investors, just think of the volatility as an opportunity, not a red flag. The progress being made by the company across all the financial, technological, and strategic initiatives demonstrated in the investor presentation suggests that the marketplace will ultimately be forced to understand the fundamental value of the company, especially when we start to see the compounding effects of its royalty-driven model.

Valuation

For Arteris valuation, I've used a Comparable Company Analysis using a forward Enterprise Value-to-sales (EV/sales) multiple, as this is the most appropriate methodology for a high growth, not-yet-profitable company in the software/IP domain. A DCF, would be much less reliable at this point, given the challenges in estimating the exact timing of the royalty streams, and the timing of Arteris path to positive free cash flow.

I'm assuming the following key concepts as a foundation for my valuation:

Revenue Growth: Given the strong pipeline comments and significant impact of successful AI design wins, I believe a forward revenue estimate of $75 million (next 12 months) is reasonable. This estimate is an extension of company guidance of $66-$70 million for full year 2025, which implies an approximate 30% bottom line growth from the previous period. I think that the ramp is warranted given the momentum gained from new AI partnerships, particularly with AMD, and a record high Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO) backlog that indicate revenue will be ramping up as these significant contracts have a greater impact over the course of the following quarters.

Peer Group: The two premier semiconductor IP and EDA (Electronic Design Automation) categories of players are Synopsys (SNPS) and Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), which are large, profitable companies trading at the premium forward EV/Sales multiples from Finviz of approximately 18 (Synopsys) and 19 (Cadence); the market is giving these categories the premium multiples due to the mission-critical software and sticky customer relationship they have developed.

Valuation Multiple: Arteris, is smaller and does not have positive profits, so we are going to need to provide a discount to the larger industry leaders, but arguably has better growth rates. I will use a multiple of 11x of forward EV/sales, which is a discount to much larger peers and reflects the strong growth characteristics of the company.

The mathematics of calculation is:

Projected Forward Revenue: $75 million

Applied/Valuation EV/Sales Multiple: 11.0x

Calculated Enterprise Value [EV]: $825 million

To arrive at the fair value market cap, I will need to adjust for the net cash position of the company . Based on the last balance sheet of Arteris, the company has approximately $50 million in cash and little debt.

Enterprise Value: $825 million

Add: Cash-net cash: $50 million

Calculated Fair Value Market Cap: $875 million

In the current environment, Arteris has about 38 million shares outstanding, yielding a price target of approximately:

Target Price = $875 million market cap / 38 million shares = ~$23.00/share

The price target represents an upside of more than 100% relative to current valuations and points to the significant disconnect between Arteris current price in the market and its value.

Key Investment Risks

The main risks to my thesis are the following:

Competition: Arteris competes against large semiconductor companies that all have their own internal IP divisions, as well as the established EDA players such as Synopsys and Cadence, who can leverage their scale to compete.

Customer Product Delays: Arteris's royalty revenue is directly tied to the success of its customers with getting their chips to market and ramp to volume. Hence, substantial product delay from a major product like the AMD AI chiplet would delay this highly profitable source of revenue.

Timeline to Profitability: The company is in a strong financial position, however, any failure to become cash flow positive within the next 2-3 years could impact the stock and potentially require additional sources of capital.

Final Takeaway

With Arteris, we have a rare chance to invest in the core layer of the AI boom at a good value. We now have confirmation that Arteris is at the start of a sustained long-term bull market, because they are executing with the right, best-in-class technology, in a partnership with a world-class leader like AMD, and they are growing in a hyper-scalable, high margin business model direction (which means the market's short-term belief in profitability, is missing the compounding of Arteris's future royalty stream).

We stood in the same hinge moment, as now, and this is being further confirmed by the stock price technicals. The news of their partnership with AMD was huge, and the stock exploded by over +30% right away and put the company squarely on the map of a much bigger universe of further investors. This was not a "one and done" move, there was deep conviction behind this move, as evidenced in the trading volume on August 5th, when shares traded over 15x of their average volume, the highest volume ever since the IPO. This type of price action and volume is usually one of several classic signs, from a technical take, that a fundamental shift has occurred in investor thinking, and this will be the start (go signal) of the deteriorating move (multi-weeks/multi-months rally) up. That reported level is 12.40, recent highs at the end of 2024 are now supported.

A pullback on lower volume in the next few days would be great to find a low-risk entry for people who were not able to enter on the first day of this big move.

At the end of the day, the fundamental story, push-pull strategy events, and its emerging technical revenue style momentum are all aligned for a bright future. I am putting a strong "Buy" rating on the stock, with a target price $23.00.

data.title: CommScope's Leaner Strategy Supports A Cautious Buy After Major Price Surge
data.description:
About a week and a half ago, I rated CommScope Holding Company, Inc. (COMM) a buy at $8. Since then, the stock has climbed to $15.65 before settling just under $15 as of August 6, 2025. That’s an 85 percent climb in two weeks and a 550 percent gain over the past year. With numbers like these, it’s only fair to ask whether this rapid rise still has fuel, or if caution is now the smarter move.

What’s Driving This Wild Ride?

This surge isn’t just luck or market noise. CommScope has made some bold moves, including the sale of its Connectivity and Cable Solutions Segment to Amphenol for $10.5 billion. This was a massive part of their operations and included several products tied to fast-growing markets like AI. By letting it go, CommScope is now focusing more on its network access and cellular solutions units, which management believes can drive growth and fatter profit margins. The latest press releases have made it clear that CommScope is doubling down on a strategy that puts profits and focus ahead of just size.

The Company Today: Sharper Focus, Smaller Footprint

After the big sale, CommScope looks a lot leaner and more focused. They are no longer spreading themselves thin across too many business lines. Instead, they’re putting their chips on areas that can grow faster and deliver higher returns. Q2 2025 results tell the story. Sales jumped by 32 percent year-over-year to $1.39 billion.

Gross margins rose to 42.6 percent, showing better efficiency and smarter execution. Operating income almost tripled compared to the same time last year. Sure, net income was boosted by a one-off gain but even looking past that, the trend is clear. The company cut its debt by over $2 billion in just a few months, now sitting at $7.25 billion and cash on hand has climbed to $571 million. Yes, operating cash flow is still in the red but a lot of that comes from swings in working capital, not a deeper problem. Management stands by its full-year EBITDA goal of $1.15 to $1.2 billion, which is a sign they believe the new CommScope can keep up the momentum.

A Fast-Moving Industry and CommScope’s Place in It

The demand for stronger, more reliable networks is only getting bigger, thanks to AI, the cloud and all things 5G. CommScope finds itself right in the middle, with both wired and wireless offerings and deep relationships with the big telecom and cable players. If the company can hold on to its customer base and keep rolling out new, useful products, there’s a real shot to gain even more ground. News headlines point to fresh strategic partnerships and plenty of investor excitement after the asset sale and the recent earnings beat.

What the Numbers Say About Value

With the stock at $14.77, CommScope now trades at about 11 times expected earnings for 2025 and an enterprise value to EBITDA ratio of around 11. That’s no super cheap price but it isn’t too high given the strong growth and cleaner strategy. The key point is this: as the sale money comes in and goes toward paying off more debt, CommScope’s enterprise value, which adds market cap and debt together before subtracting cash, will drop. The stock will look cheaper compared to profits, even if earnings themselves do not move much right away.

If management can deliver on its goals, keep margins strong and continue to pay down debt as expected, the EV/EBITDA multiple should fall over time. The company will also have lower interest costs and more flexibility to use its cash for growth projects, stock buybacks, or even future dividends. Because of this, the recent run in the share price is about more than just excitement, it reflects real, positive changes to CommScope’s financial health and future outlook.

When you put it all together, the asset sale lets CommScope put its resources into faster-growing business lines and it also makes the current stock price look more attractive once the impact on its financial structure is fully realized. If CommScope keeps meeting its targets, it is not hard to imagine shares moving into the $16 to $18 range over the next year, which would give investors a respectable upside from where things are now.

What Could Get in the Way

Of course, not everything is smooth sailing. Debt remains high, even after the recent reduction and interest payments will still take a bite out of cash flow if things go sideways. The company is now more concentrated, which means any mistake, whether it’s losing a customer, a failed new product, or a slowdown in the industry, could hit harder than before. CommScope is also less tied to AI and the hottest areas of tech after selling the Connectivity and Cable Solutions unit. If those sectors really explode, the company may be left watching from the sidelines.

Looking Ahead: What I’m Watching Closely

The next few quarters will be key. I want to see CommScope make good on its new business plan, keep lifting margins and avoid any big surprises with cash flow or customer contracts. Analyst estimates are generally positive and management talks a good game but what happens next on the ground will matter more than any guidance or projection.

How the Future Might Play Out

If things go wrong, maybe the smaller CommScope stumbles and can’t keep up the pace, which could mean the stock gives up some of its gains, especially if the broader market turns rocky. The most likely scenario, in my mind, is that the company hits its goals, keeps growing profit, and the stock moves higher but at a slower, steadier pace. If everything works out, CommScope might land new deals or grab back some AI and data center work, and that could open the door to even higher prices.

Wrapping It Up: Still A Buy but Be Thoughtful

So where do I land? I’m sticking with a buy, but with a much more careful approach than before. For new investors, I’d wait for dips closer to $13 or $14 to start a position. The company’s outlook is brighter, but there’s less room for mistakes. If management delivers, there’s still value here. This isn’t a high-risk bet anymore, but for anyone who wants a stake in a sharper, faster CommScope and is willing to keep a close watch, it deserves a spot on your radar.

data.title: Macy's Is More Than Its Real Estate, But It Needs To Be Bolder
data.description:
It’s no secret that Wall Street has long recognized Macy’s, Inc. (NYSE:M) as a real estate play masquerading as a retailer. There have been multiple attempts at private equity takeovers reminiscent of Sears and Toys"R"Us (which, coincidentally, has come back via Macy’s department stores). The most recent attempt was Arkhouse and Brigade Capital’s $6.6 billion activist proposal in 2024, which saw a failed buyout bid of $24.80 per share in June (a whopping 30% premium to its stock price), as well as the successful addition of two Arkhouse advisors to the Macy’s board. But while its physical assets are undoubtedly valuable, Macy’s true upside lies in unlocking both its properties and its retail model. Given the lukewarm shareholder response to its Bold New Chapter strategy that feels more reactive than revolutionary (Macy’s stock price is down 35% since its February 2024 announcement), private equity may wind up being the catalyst that Macy’s needs for meaningful change.

What is not reflected in Macy’s stock price is its cultural capital. From the classic movie Miracle on 34th Street to its iconic Thanksgiving Day Parade, Macy’s is at the heart of American retail history and culture. Its Herald Square property, the crown jewel of American department stores, is a whopping 2.5 million square feet, half of which is occupied by retail space. Its self-proclaimed moniker, “The World’s Largest Department Store,” holds value for New Yorkers and tourists alike. The property is also worth between $1 billion and $3 billion, a well-known fact that keeps private equity knocking at the door. Pre-Covid, Macy’s sold its San Francisco men’s store for $250 million, its Minneapolis Flagship for $59 million, a portion of its Chicago flagship for $27 million, its Pittsburgh flagship for $15 million, and shuttered over 100 other doors. Despite these major closures, Macy’s real estate holdings today are still worth at least double the company’s market cap of $3.6 billion. In fact, Macy’s entire real estate portfolio is estimated to be worth between $7.9 billion and $10.5 billion as of Spring 2024, according to Brandon Svec, national director of US retail analytics for CoStar Group. That’s mouth-watering on its own, but its overlooked brand value could make the company worth so much more if it could only be revitalized the right way. Yet from Wall Street’s perspective, the company’s only real value lies in what things other than a retail location a Macy’s can be turned into. The better question is, has Macy’s just lost its way, and does it really need to be something it’s not? New leadership under CEO Tony Spring, at the helm since February 2024, attempts to answer that question while trying to steer the ship in the right direction. So far, though, the early efforts have not been enough to inspire confidence, and the company is setting itself up for another takeover attempt.

As part of the Bold New Chapter, the company asked over 60,000 current and former customers what they want from their shopping experience, attempting to focus on how to breathe life back into the business. Shoppers want a better merchandise assortment, a better service experience, a better visual experience, a modern take on marketing, and a quicker supply chain, what Mr. Spring calls, “Some of the fundamentals of retail.” But here’s the kicker. Rebranding the fundamentals of retail as ‘bold’ and ‘new’ suggests a reactionary stance, rather than the kind of transformational positioning that invites the types of innovations needed to keep Macy’s relevant in an ever-evolving retail landscape. And that starts with its real estate. Let’s return to Herald Square for a moment. Its big-box, department store stature heaves over the landscape between 7th Avenue and Broadway next to the newly renovated Penn Station. It’s the perfect spot for the bridge-and-tunnel suburbanites seeking the big-city experience, daily commuters waiting for their trains, tourists entering Midtown Manhattan, and locals alike. Inside the store, however, is a maze of (constantly broken) escalators and merchandise with no real sense of direction or a centralized location for shoppers to orient themselves. In essence, it’s missing the equivalent of shopping feng shui. Compare that to the open concepts and architectural beauty of the Galeries Lafayette in Paris or the Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II in Milan, each with domed glass ceilings and Renaissance-style uniformity in cities that are global fashion capitals. They give individual shops and vendors the space to ooze their own unique style, creating brand equity (and justifiable lease costs) while also keeping things neat, tidy, and navigable for the consumer. Part of what made Macy’s so iconic is that the American department store was a huge part of American culture. Things are different now. Enclosed spaces with escalators and fluorescent lighting do not make for great TikToks or shareable Instagram posts, and people want to navigate experiential locations (think the High Line or the Oculus). Culture has shifted, and private equity is right to a certain degree: real estate is a huge part of that change, but it does not have to mean turning a Macy’s mall anchor into mixed-use apartments, restaurants, and theaters. Instead, it could mean something like transforming a windowless box into an open-air marketplace concept, replete with all the brands Macy’s is known for. On the other hand, while investing in refreshing visuals and modern marketing is a step in the right direction, it’s not the same as tearing down and rebuilding a national flagship to create a more fashionable cultural landmark in a city known for its fashion, culture, and landmarks.

At the crux of the Bold New Chapter turnaround strategy is a story investors and customers have seen in the past: shuttering stores (150 by 2027), focusing on growing its smaller format market by Macy’s locations, expanding its luxury footprint (Macy’s owns Bloomingdale’s and Bluemercury), and allocating capital to its remaining fleet. For context, in 2020, the company announced it would cut 125 stores over three years to focus on opening smaller formats, expand its off-price offering (Macy’s Backstage), and focus its capital on the remaining fleet. For even more context, in 2016, Macy’s announced it would close 100 stores to expand Backstage, Bloomingdale’s, and Bluemercury, while investing capital into its remaining fleet. Notice the pattern? The company has rinsed and repeated nearly identical strategies for a decade. For comparison’s sake, in 2016, Macy’s FY sales were $25.8 billion, gross margin was 39.4%, and operating income was 5.1% of revenue. Fast-forward to 2024 and hundreds of store closures later: FY sales were $22.3 billion, gross margin was 38.4%, and operating income was 4%. Note that sales are not comparable, as it is impossible to calculate without knowing which stores were operating and their individual performance. However, “return to growth” implies that the remaining fleet and the website should generate enough sales to offset lost revenue from the shuttered doors, while margin and operating income should improve. These attempts at change haven’t moved the needle over the last decade and within the last year. Below is 2024 FY Comparable Growth for Macy’s, some of its competitors, and the total retail industry excluding gas and groceries. Macy’s fell behind the market in the first big year of its new strategy.

Macy’s isn’t blind to retail evolution either. It has adopted omnichannel tactics, loyalty programs, and edits to market and private brand assortments like many of its competitors have. Yet, execution seems to lack the cohesion and emotional resonance needed to attract the next generation of customers, particularly Millennials and Gen Z. This is common amongst department stores, and it could be a symptom of many things: wrong assortments, not catching or creating trends, and marketing tactics that fail to meet target customers where they are. The major concern is that even if Macy’s can capture some Gen Z market share now, will it be able to retain those customers as they age? This is where there’s a retail identity crisis. By trying to be something for everyone, Macy’s risks becoming nothing for anyone.

Part of this disconnect is the rise of retailers like Amazon (AMZN), Shein, Temu (PDD), and Zara (ITX), which are all fashion-forward and inexpensive. Younger generations know that they do not need to spend a lot of money to look trendy, find products for their homes, or go on a treasure hunt for niche goods. Combined with the convenience of e-commerce, young shoppers can easily find exactly what they want at the right price while at the same time understanding that they might be taking a risk on quality, and they are ok with that. Macy’s carries a solid assortment of high-quality, and thus higher-priced, legacy vendors such as Polo Ralph Lauren (RL) and private brands such as INC, Bar III, and Alfani, but it lacks the speed-to-market that can deliver fashion quickly and affordably the way a company like Zara can, especially in today’s world where trends come and go within weeks. Instead, the company plans to expand its luxury footprint. Bloomingdale’s and Bluemercury year-over-year sales results often outperform the Macy’s nameplate, and the company is chasing that market. But even luxury is not immune to downtrends. The growth in luxury over the last decade was spurred by price increases, and the industry is now grappling with lower consumer demand due to tariffs, recession fears, and price-point alienation. Aspirational Millennials and Zoomers will undoubtedly be left out of the market in the near term, and Macy’s nameplate will need to do the heavy lifting to fill the void. This is a rare opportunity for the company to capture share amongst the traditional and occasional luxury customers who are now trading down due to economic concerns. With the right marketing, capital, and infrastructure behind it, Macy’s private brand portfolio could be a fashion-forward, trend-setting juggernaut if it could adopt Zara’s business model: a vertically integrated supply chain that can bring runway-inspired products to market in a matter of weeks instead of months, limiting inventory to create urgency, and generating high product turnover and margin. Some of the company’s unproductive retail space can house design studios and concept stores that can create ultra-niche products for location-specific markets, creating a culture of creative innovation spanning across the country. Local trends can easily bubble up to the national level without buyers in a central office having to scour social media for the next best thing (plus, once something trends on TikTok, it’s already too late to hop on). Customers will be delighted with fresh assortments that keep them coming back for more, which will increase foot traffic and cross-divisional sales opportunities.

Unproductive stores do not need to close; they can be reimagined as fresh spaces without sacrificing too much of the core business. As for the assortment strategy, the company will benefit from modernizing its private brands for the next generation, increasing speed-to-market, keeping pace with the latest runways, and delivering an experience that feels luxurious at accessible prices. Private equity has the rigor, potential, and capital to force that kind of revamp. There are opportunities to streamline SKUs, invest in better store and product design, and take bold risks that the company so far has not been brave enough to do. Yes, PE firms can still extract value from real estate via sale-leasebacks or REIT spinoffs. However, it’s more important that the company and the Street meet in the middle and create something revolutionary. Why not put a giant Ferris Wheel in Herald Square like Toys"R"Us had in its former Times Square location? Shopping is not just about shopping anymore. It’s about the thrill of the hunt and elevated experiences that blend the real world with our ever-expanding digital world. It’s about generating excitement that makes the consumer want to share their “fit” on social media against a scenic backdrop, and those “Aha!” moments when you find exactly what you’re looking for in a venue that’s worth exploring.

As it stands, truly bold changes hinge on another private equity takeover attempt because the company’s 2025 outlook is not compelling enough to believe that investors and customers are entering a new era at Macy’s. Nothing is guaranteed, but if history repeats itself, another bid may come. The company is expected to release Q2 earnings on August 20th, and much of the same is anticipated. Total revenue and net sales are projected to be -4.8% to last year, with gross margin dropping -11% based on the company’s FY 2025 negative 30-70 basis point projection. Economic headwinds such as inflation, tariffs, and interest rates are pinching consumers, so do not be surprised if results are lower. The consumer is looking for deals, if they’re looking at all, and that hits profitability. Not to mention the impact that the drop in tourism will have on key locations. Merchandise inventory, difficult to project, could remain in line with Q1’s trend of -.5% if the company stays disciplined, but ideally it will fall more in line with the company’s projected sales trend to increase turnover and avoid overstocks.

Based on the company’s own projected margin rate for FY 2025, EBITDA is expected to land -16% to last year (Q1 was -11%). This is after the company, in its Q1 2024 presentation, said it was targeting mid-single-digit EBITDA growth as part of its Bold New Chapter. Using Q2 2024’s interest expense, effective tax rate (23%), depreciation, and amortization as a guide, EBIT should be $156M, and pre-tax income is projected to be $129M. Therefore, net income should be $99M, an improvement from Q1 but -33% to last year. Company guidance on earnings per share is between $.15 and $.20. However, Macy’s is usually known for surprise earnings. Last quarter, the company projected $.14 per share but reported $.16 per share, a 14% surprise. It’s possible that EPS for Q2 could achieve $.35 per share, which falls more in line with average analyst estimates of $.45 per share.

Operating cash flow is volatile as it largely depends on inventories and accounts payable swings. Based on 2022-2024 data, on average, there is a $70M increase in operating cash flow between Q1 and Q2, although it varies by year. Taking this into account, OCF is projected at $6M, an improvement from -$64M in Q1. The company projects $800M in capital expenditures this year and has already spent $177M in Q1, focusing on digital technology capabilities and omni-channel infrastructure. Assuming $200M in Q2 capex, free cash flow is projected at -$194M versus $244M last year.

The company ended Q1 with $932M in cash and equivalents. In July, it announced a $175M cash tender offer to bondholders, which is a positive step towards managing interest and strengthening the balance sheet in the long term. That would put Q2 cash and equivalents around $563M versus $646M last year, a 13% drop. Cash is being burned rather quickly, putting pressure on future liquidity in the event of disappointing sales, supply chain disruptions, and other unexpected costs. However, the expenditures are likely part of the Bold New Chapter. Investors should take note of which areas of the business the company is targeting.

Currently, Macy’s is trading at $13, which is inexpensive compared to Arkhouse’s previous offer. EV/EBITDA is 4.69 compared to competitors Ross (ROST), TJ Maxx (TJX), and Burlington (BURL), who are north of 15. P/E is 6.58, and forward P/E is 7.19. With projected 2025 EPS guidance between $2.05 and $2.25, the market is not deeply undervaluing the company based on earnings alone. Coupled with declining sales revenue, it is hard to make the case for a value thesis as the company’s near-term growth outlook isn’t compelling. This raises serious questions about leadership effectiveness and could reignite activist interest. With untapped real estate value and no improvement in sales or earnings guidance for this fiscal year, frustrated investors may feel compelled to push for change. That’s why Macy’s stock is a solid hold and a potential buy for contrarian investors. Eventually, private equity will realize that it won’t get control of the company by circling in on its real estate. It just needs to wait for this “Bold New Chapter” to play out a little while longer until shareholders and customers alike realize that it’s not bold enough. Precedent for a stock buyout at a 30% premium to share price has been set, and investors now have a benchmark for what a takeover might look like.

In the end, it’s about business just as much as it’s about human connection to the things that consumers value: special occasions, gift-giving, anticipation, joy, and community. With so many cultural connections and the globally recognized red star logo, Macy’s should carry on as a retailer, but the brand needs major revitalization far beyond what has been proposed. The only thing stopping the company is itself. Wall Street is right in that Macy’s holds some very valuable real estate in prime markets; that’s only half of the story. The rest of it is being written, but the next chapter needs to be bolder.

data.title: Baron Emerging Markets Fund Q2 2025 Top Contributors And Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Baron Emerging Markets Fund Q2 2025 Shareholder Letter

Top Contributors & Detractors

Top contributors to performance for the quarter

Contribution to Return (%)
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) 2.71
HD Korea Shipbuilding & Offshore Engineering Co., Ltd. 2.35
HD Hyundai Heavy Industries Co., Ltd. 1.08
Doosan Enerbility Co., Ltd. 0.72
Hanwha Systems Co., Ltd. 0.61

Semiconductor giant Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSMC) contributed to performance during the second quarter, driven by robust demand for AI chips and easing investor concerns about tariffs and macroeconomic risks. We retain conviction that TSMC’s technological leadership, pricing power, and exposure to secular growth markets—including AI and high-performance computing, automotive, 5G, and Internet of Things—will allow the company to sustain strong double-digit earnings growth over the next several years.

HD Korea Shipbuilding & Offshore Engineering Co., Ltd. is the shipbuilding and offshore engineering sub-holding company of HD Hyundai, overseeing key affiliates including HHI, a leading global shipbuilder and engine manufacturer. Shares rose during the quarter on improving profit margin visibility, backlog growth, and increasing evidence of a sizable long-term opportunity in the naval and defense business. The stock also benefited from broader strength in Korean equities—particularly among chaebol-affiliated companies (large family-controlled conglomerates)—following the Korean presidential election outcome, which is expected to lead to shareholder-friendly corporate governance reforms. We continue to hold shares and remain confident in the company's long-term outlook.

HD Hyundai Heavy Industries Co., Ltd. is a leading global shipbuilder and engine manufacturer. Shares rose during the quarter on improving profit margin visibility, backlog growth, and increasing evidence of a sizable long-term opportunity in the naval and defense business, including potential partnerships with the U.S. military. HHI also benefited from broader strength in Korean equities—particularly among chaebol-affiliated companies (large family-controlled conglomerates)—following the Korean presidential election outcome, which is expected to lead to shareholder-friendly corporate governance reforms. We continue to hold shares and remain confident in the company's long-term outlook.

Top detractors from performance for the quarter

Contribution to Return (%)
Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) (0.67)
Meituan (OTCPK:MPNGF) (0.34)
Full Truck Alliance Co. Ltd. (YMM) (0.23)
China Mengniu Dairy Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:CIADF) (0.19)
Sigma Lithium Corporation (SGML) (0.19)

Alibaba Group Holding Limited is the largest retailer and e-commerce company in China. Alibaba operates shopping platforms Taobao and Tmall, and has businesses spanning logistics, local services, digital media, and cloud computing. Shares of Alibaba were pressured by quarterly results that were solid but fell short of elevated investor expectations, as well as heightened macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty impacting international stocks following Liberation Day. Alibaba is actively pursuing Artificial General Intelligence, ramping its capital expenditures over the next three years to build out its cloud infrastructure layer and add AI capabilities to existing apps (e.g., consumer search). Within its commerce business, the core market is showing continued positive signs of stabilization, and improved profitability should follow. We retain conviction that Alibaba is well positioned to benefit from China's ongoing growth in e-commerce and cloud, although competitive concerns remain.

Meituan is China’s largest food delivery platform and the leading super app for local services. Shares declined during the quarter amid intensifying domestic competition and increased spending on international expansion. Earlier this year, JD.com (JD)—a major Chinese e-commerce company—entered the food delivery space, triggering an industry-wide subsidy war. Meituan and Alibaba’s Ele.me (the second-largest platform in the industry) rolled out aggressive discounts to defend share across food delivery and the fast-growing Quick Commerce segment. Despite Meituan’s deep logistics network, robust technology stack, and proven operational expertise, these initiatives pressured near-term earnings as the company increased support for merchants and couriers. At the same time, Meituan is investing to build its presence in new markets such as the Middle East and Brazil. While we trimmed our position in light of these near-term headwinds, we continue to view Meituan as one of China’s most competitively advantaged and efficiently managed consumer internet companies, with significant long-term growth potential overseas.

Full Truck Alliance Co. Ltd. (FTA), China’s leading digital freight platform, detracted from performance during the quarter. While FTA delivered strong quarterly results, with solid revenue growth and margin improvement, it reported muted earnings guidance and revised its full-year profit forecast lower to reflect increased AI investment and anticipated losses from planned acquisitions, which disappointed the market. Shares were also pressured by growing concerns over the potential delisting of FTA’s American Depositary Receipts from U.S. exchanges, fueled in part by fears that the Trump administration could take a harder stance on Chinese listings. This is particularly concerning for FTA, which does not yet have a Hong Kong listing, though the company is actively preparing for one. We retain conviction in FTA’s growth outlook, supported by expanding market share, higher take rate potential, and long-term benefits from its strategic investments in AI and logistics technology.

data.title: BB Seguridade Q2: Sustained Pressure Slows Near-Term Growth
data.description:
I am downgrading BB Seguridade (OTCPK:BBSEY) from Hold to Sell after the company reported its 2Q25 results on August 5.

Recurring net income came in strong in the most recent quarter, up nearly 20% year-over-year and 12% quarter-over-quarter, validating the company's efficiency despite some persistent operational hiccups. The bigger problem here was the downward revision of guidance, especially at Brasilseg and Brasilprev, which limits enthusiasm and suggests a very weak H2 ahead, in which I believe it may continue the underperformance trend seen in H1.

Although in the long term the thesis remains compelling, with relatively attractive dividend yields (considering country risk), my bearishness is based on a six-month outlook. Given valuations don't look particularly attractive, I have my doubts as to whether there's any margin of safety sufficient to cover a more conservative outlook ahead, considering a scenario of weaker growth at Banco do Brasil (OTCPK:BDORY) (parent company), which tends to impact Brasilseg's brokerage revenues and premiums earned.

Recapping the Thesis

BB Seguridade has five core segments, with the following characteristics:

  • Brasilseg: very high relevance in the P&L, with medium risk and growth levers in Banco do Brasil credit and agribusiness.
  • Brasilprev: high relevance in the P&L, with low risk and interest rates and funding as growth levers.
  • BB Broker: also highly relevant to the P&L, but with low risk, with Banco do Brasil sales as the main driver.
  • Brasilcap and Brasildental: less relevant segments, with medium risk (more volatile results) and low risk (depending on base growth), respectively.

In May, right after the company reported its 1Q25 results, I wrote the following concerning BB Seguridade:

Despite the recent hiccups experienced in 1Q25 for BB Seguridade due to weak volume growth numbers—indexing potential demand weakness—profitability remains solid due to growth in net investment income, as a consequence of high interest rates in the country. And consequently, stable guidance for 2025 provides good stability for the dividend thesis, which remains above 9% for 2025."

Among these hiccups, the weaker performance of its most representative division, Brasilseg (insurance unit), stood out, where it saw weakness in sequential purchase down by (12.4%) in adjusted income, a consequence of the drop in premium writing, which fell in 1Q24 (6.5%) QoQ and (5.9%) YoY.

Thus, based on Brasilseg's guidance for premiums written for 2025, the (5.9%) reported in 1Q25 fell well short of the 2% to 7% initially projected. What saved the day was non-interest operating results growing by 4.2% in 1Q25, still within the initial projections of 3% to 8%—but largely due to the robust contribution of the financial results of Brasilseg and Brasilprev, which are still benefiting from the high interest rate environment (Selic currently at 15%), which favors the performance of floating-rate portfolios.

On BB Seguridade's 2Q25 Earnings

In 2Q25, looking at the full picture, the result is not bad.

Recurring net income was R$2.24 billion, up 12.2% QoQ and 19.7% YoY, in a performance driven by strong financial results (once again benefiting from the high interest rate scenario) and an improvement in claims in the insurance segment. Another positive point is that all four core segments (Brasilseg, Brasilprev, Brasilcap, and BB Broker) saw sequential profit growth, something that had not been seen since 2023.

Brasilseg was the main highlight, with a 25.5% YoY and 17.1% QoQ increase in profitability against the backdrop of high interest rates, and also positively impacted by a non-recurring effect of R$129 million, resulting from the reversal of legal provisions after a legislative change. It also performed well in terms of claims, with declines of (4.8 pp QoQ) and (8.8 pp YoY), helping to strengthen the bottom line.

Brasilprev reported net income jumping 17% QoQ and 19.8% YoY, also driven by financial results and a favorable effect of IGP-M (Brazil's General Market Price Index) deflation on the cost of liabilities—as a significant portion of Brasilprev's liabilities is linked to this index, reducing the monetary correction of obligations.

But when projecting what lies ahead, the scenario points to significant structural challenges. The same hiccups from 1Q25 not only persisted but worsened. Difficulties in expanding insurance premiums and continuing to record negative net inflows in pension plans forced a downward revision in guidance for 2025.

On the operational side, Brasilseg's premiums written fell (7.6%) QoQ and (0.5%) YoY, with pressure mainly in the life and agricultural segments, with a quarterly decline of (11%) and an annual decline of (22.9%), reflecting a scenario of contraction in rural credit from Banco do Brasil—a consequence of climatic events, very high interest rates, and increased delinquency.

For Brasilprev, gross pension contributions fell (27%) QoQ and (21.6%) YoY, pressured by reduced funding after the federal decree that began taxing IOF (Brazilian financial transaction tax) on pension plans for monthly contributions above R$50,000 from the end of May—although this was later revoked to R$300,000.

Even with figures generally in line with consensus in 2Q25, these red flags mentioned above in operational terms led BB Seguridade to revise its annual guidance downward.

The expected growth in written premiums went from 2% to 7% to (4%) to 1%, operating income was adjusted from 3% to 8% to 1% to 4%, and pension plan reserve growth fell from 12% to 16% to 9% to 12%. Ouch!

On Dividends

With consensus pointing to revenues of $1.81 billion (approximately R$9.95 billion) and considering a net income margin of 84.5%, net income would be $1.53 billion or R$8.4 billion. My past projections anticipated a net income of ~R$9 billion for this year.

If BB Seguridade's payout continues to range between ~80% and 90%, as hinted by the management team, it is still possible to project, at the lower end, US$1.2 billion in dividends, which at the current share price of $6.14, and assuming no changes in shares outstanding, the yield would be ~10-11%—higher than my previous projections of ~9%-10%. It is important to note that this is due to the decline in the ADR price from $6.54 to $6.20 since I made my initial projections.

I do not see this as an advantage in this case because the higher yield does not come from operational improvements but rather from the share price decline.

The slower growth, confirmed by more conservative guidance, shows that, in my view, the market may be right to punish BBSEY a little.

I also reiterate the issue of country risk. BBSEY's dividends come from an emerging asset exposed to fiscal risks, inflation, and exchange rate volatility. The 10Y U.S. Treasury has yielded ~4.3%, and real interest rates in Brazil (Selic minus inflation) are 5% per year, well above those in the U.S. Furthermore, even if BBSEY pays 10% in dividends in USD today, any devaluation of the BRL can erode the real return, as the company's profits are in BRL and converted to USD.

For example, as we saw in 2024, the BRL fell more than 20% against the USD, and in that scenario, much of the 8.5% dividend yield in 2024 was arguably wiped out.

On Valuations

Updating the Residual Income Model (RIM), based on an increase in ROE from 1Q25 to 2Q25 from 74% to 79% (driven by a sequential increase in stockholders equity of 30% reflecting strong net income), considering the same book value per share (BVPS) of $1.05, cost of capital (Ke) of ~16% (Selic rate + 0.35 beta + 5% ERP), and long-term growth rate (g) of 3%, the implied price for BBSEY is $6.14, when the ADR currently trades at $6.20.

However, I believe that, given the weaker guidance and structural headwinds in Banco do Brasil's credit scenario, which are affecting BB Seguridade's written premiums, the impact may still be even more pessimistic going forward, implying no clear margin of safety, especially taken into consideration the additional following concerns: (1) risk of being a state-owned company; (2) FX fluctuations; (3) interest rates eventually reaching a peak; (4) country risk.

Risks and Final Remarks

The main risk of being bearish on BBSEY is that it is a defensive company with very solid fundamentals and, even with its operations under pressure, it still partially offsets its consolidated results based on (1) extraordinarily high margins; (2) good predictability in recurring revenues; (3) historically high payouts; and (4) a strong relationship with Banco do Brasil, which, even facing weaker credit cycles, still guarantees massive distribution of the insurer's products.

However, I am pessimistic in the short to midterm about the persistent challenges in 2Q25 versus the previous quarter, such as (1) a sequential decline in premiums written by Brasilseg, suggesting a loss of commercial traction and potentially competitive pressure; (2) lower-than-expected growth in Brasilprev's reserves; (3) a weaker credit cycle at Banco do Brasil, especially in rural areas, affecting agricultural insurance; (4) valuation not so discounted in the face of escalating risks putting pressure on the safety margin; and finally (5) the relevant exchange rate risk for foreign investors, as any devaluation of the BRL could nullify much of the nominal dividend yield.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Tariff Win Sends TSMC Higher
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

TSMC climbs to record levels as Taiwan says chip giant exempt from Trump tariffs. (00:23) Samsung (SSLNF) shares rise as Apple taps Texas factory for chip supply. (01:10) United Airlines (UAL) resumes operations after technical glitch. (02:15)

This is an abridged transcript.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) clinched a record high on Thursday after Taiwan said its top chip manufacturer will not have to pay a 100% tariff on such imports to the US.

Taipei-listed TSMC jumped nearly 5% to 1180 won as the time of this recording.

U.S. President Donald Trump said Wednesday he plans to impose a 100% tariff on semiconductor imports with an exemption for companies that manufacture or commit to manufacturing in the U.S.

TSMC has announced significant investments in the U.S recently, including an initial $65 billion to build three plants in Arizona, and an additional $100 billion U.S. investment in March.

Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) climbed on Thursday after Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) said it would source chips from the company's Texas factory.

“Apple is also working with Samsung at its fab in Austin, Texas, to launch an innovative new technology for making chips, which has never been used before anywhere in the world. By bringing this technology to the U.S. first, this facility will supply chips that optimize power and performance of Apple products, including iPhone devices shipped all over the world," the company said in a statement.

If you remember last week SA told you that Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk confirmed that the EV maker has signed a $16.5 billion semiconductor supply deal with Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF).

In a separate development, South Korea’s top trade envoy Yeo Han-koo said on Thursday that Samsung Electronics and SK hynix (OTCPK:HXSCF) will not be subject to 100% U.S. tariffs on chips.

Seoul-listed Samsung shares rose as much as 3.2% to 71,000 won.

United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL) said that the technical issue that led to over 1,000 flights delayed and dozens canceled across U.S. airports has been resolved.

The airline faced a glitch on Wednesday that affected its computer systems—specifically a problem with the airline's weight and balance computer system.

United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL) reported the system outage began after 6 p.m. ET. The Federal Aviation Administration halted its departures at several major hub airports, including Chicago, Houston, Denver, Newark, and San Francisco.

According to company statements, the problem was not related to a cyberattack.

The malfunction was resolved later that evening, allowing the airline to resume departures. However, the carrier warned of continued residual delays as staff worked through the backlog and rerouted stranded passengers.

According to Flight Aware, United has canceled 134 flights for today, as of the time of this recording.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

The Week After: How has MSFT performed since FQ4 earnings?

AppLovin outlines 20–30% annual growth target and expands AXON platform globally as e-commerce ramps

IonQ outlines $1.6B cash position and targets 80,000 logical qubits by 2030 while expanding global quantum footprint

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 0.5% at $64/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.1% at $114,000. Gold is up 0.3% at $3,379.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.2% and the DAX is up 1.7%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: e.l.f. Beauty (NYSE:ELF) -11% – Shares dropped after the cosmetics company reported Q2 results and issued cautious guidance for the first half of the fiscal year.
